title: No Resource, No Benchmarks, No Problem? Evaluating and Improving LLMs for Code Generation in No-Resource Languages

claims.claim_text: No-resource languages for which LLMs have seen virtually no training data remain largely unstudied.

evidence.quote: In contrast, no-resource languages for which LLMs have seen virtually no training data remain largely unstudied.
little attention has been paid to no-resource programming languages, i.e., languages that fall outside the pre-training distribution of LLMs.

claims.claim_text: Further pre-training gives the largest performance gains for no-resource languages in RQ2, but applying it directly to instruction-tuned models harms their ability to follow instructions.

evidence.quote: While further pre-training gives the largest performance gains for no-resource languages, applying it directly to instruction-tuned models harms their ability to follow instructions.

pre-training is the most promising technique since, differently from fine-tuning, allows to exploit 'all' little data available for the no-resource language, achieving the strongest boost in performance among all experimented techniques.

claims.claim_text: Weight diff transfer (instruction transferring) significantly improves code generation capabilities in no-resource settings, allowing companies to cheaply deploy specialized instruct models without instruction fine-tuning cost.

evidence.quote: start from a base model, further pre-training it on the target language, and then inject instruction-following capabilities via weight diff transfer from an instruction model. Such an approach significantly improves code generation capabilities in no-resource settings.
Instruction transferring yields a significant improvement over the base model further pre-trained on the no-resource language, with pass@1 scores increasing by up to 33%

claims.claim_text: Gleam and MoonBit have at least one order of magnitude fewer GitHub repositories than the least popular low-resource language (Racket).

evidence.quote: MoonBit (no-resource) 400, Gleam (no-resource) 2,900, Racket (low-resource) 22,200
they have at least one order of magnitude less GitHub repositories as compared to the least popular low-resource language (Racket)

claims.claim_text: Before March 2024 (cutoff for 4 of 6 LLMs), only 560 Gleam and 7 MoonBit GitHub repositories existed.

evidence.quote: before March 2024 only 560 Gleam and 7 MoonBit GitHub repositories existed. This is a relevant date since, as we will explain later, it is the latest cutoff date for four of the LLMs considered in our study

claims.claim_text: On McEval-Hard (most challenging benchmark), LLMs achieve 59-89% pass@1 for high-resource, 27-84% for low-resource, and 0-1% for no-resource languages.

evidence.quote: The four models achieve pass@1 scores being in the range of ~59-89% for high-resource languages, 27-84% for low-resource, and 0-1% for no-resource.
All studied LLMs exhibit high performance when evaluated on high-resource programming languages. The pass@1 obtained for these languages ranges between 59% and 97%

claims.claim_text: Low-resource language performance is not solely determined by their popularity (number of GitHub repositories).

evidence.quote: the distribution of results in Table IV demonstrates that the performance of low-resource languages is not solely determined by their popularity. For instance, LLMs consistently perform better on Lua than on R, although the former has a smaller number of GitHub repositories compared to the latter

claims.claim_text: The vast majority of failures on no-resource languages are due to syntactic errors (~2/3 for best model GPT-4o, up to 90% for o3-mini on Gleam).

evidence.quote: in Gleam and MoonBit the vast majority of failures are due to syntactic errors, indicating that LLMs struggle with the basic syntax of these languages. For instance, for the best-performing model on these languages (i.e., GPT-4o), roughly two-thirds of the failures are syntactic. This proportion is even higher for other models (e.g., up to 90% for o3-mini on Gleam).

claims.claim_text: In high- and low-resource languages, syntactic errors represent a minority of failures (typically below 10%), except Java (~30%).

evidence.quote: in the high- and low-resource languages, syntactic errors represent a minority of the failures (typically below 10%), suggesting that LLMs generally possess a solid knowledge of their syntax. A notable exception is Java, which consistently exhibits a higher fraction of syntactic failures (~30%).

claims.claim_text: Few-shot prompting is slightly more effective than RAG for no-resource languages (7/12 cases Gleam, 8/12 MoonBit).

evidence.quote: few-shot is slightly more effective than RAG. Indeed, in 7 out of 12 cases for Gleam and 8 out of 12 for MoonBit, few-shot outperforms RAG in terms of pass@1

claims.claim_text: Fine-tuning is superior to in-context learning for the same models on no-resource languages.

evidence.quote: given the same models, fine-tuning is superior to in-context learning. Also, fine-tuned open models are competitive with commercial ones used with few-shot or RAG.

claims.claim_text: For the same architecture (Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B), further pre-training outperforms fine-tuning on all benchmarks and languages.

evidence.quote: On all benchmarks and for both languages, the pre-trained base model is superior to the fine-tuned instruct model. The gap in performance is major in all cases.
For Gleam: on HumanEval, 32.99 (pre-trained) vs 24.74 (fine-tuned); on MBPP, 47.35 vs 34.03; and on McEval-Hard, 12.47 vs 3.04.

claims.claim_text: Pre-training benefits from larger available data (28.3M Gleam tokens) vs fine-tuning (3.6M Gleam tokens) because it can use code files and documentation, not just functions with docstrings.

evidence.quote: when pre-training, the entire code files as well as any source of language documentation can be used for teaching the language to the model. Instead, fine-tuning requires the building of natural language descriptions of the code to implement paired with a corresponding code implementation.
Pre-training Gleam 28.3M total tokens, MoonBit 13.7M; Fine-tuning Gleam 3.6M, MoonBit 0.5M

claims.claim_text: Instruction transferring yields significant improvement over pre-training (avg +12% pass@1), with OR ranging from 1.21 to 10.95.

evidence.quote: Instruction transferring yields a significant improvement over the base model further pre-trained on the no-resource language, with pass@1 scores increasing by up to 33% (Gleam, HumanEval, Qwen 3), and an average increase across benchmarks, models and languages of 12%.
All improvements are statistically significant (adjusted p-value < 0.05, McNemar test), with an OR ranging between 1.21 and 10.95.

claims.claim_text: Qwen 3 8B with instruction transferring outperforms Qwen 3 32B Instruct in any configuration (fine-tuned or in-context learning).

evidence.quote: Qwen 3 8B Base complemented with instruction transferring consistently outperforms Qwen 3 32B Instruct, whatever technique is applied on it.

up to +28% (measured in absolute terms) increments in pass@1 scores over the fine-tuned version of Qwen 3 32B Instruct (Gleam, HumanEval), with an average increase of 12% across all benchmarks and languages.

claims.claim_text: Instruction transferring generalizes across different model sizes (8B vs 32B), types (code-specialized vs general-purpose), and with/without reasoning capabilities.

evidence.quote: This boost is observed across different languages, benchmarks, and models. More importantly, it generalizes to models having different sizes (8B vs 32B), being general-purpose (Qwen 3) or specialized on code (Qwen 2.5 Coder), and with or without reasoning capabilities (Qwen 3 vs Qwen 2.5 Coder).

claims.claim_text: Only 4% of translated prompts and 0.6% of tests in the benchmarks had quality issues after manual validation.

evidence.quote: 14 prompts (4%) and two tests (0.6%) presenting issues

claims.claim_text: The fine-tuning datasets have high quality across three dimensions (content adequacy, conciseness, fluency) with median scores of 5/5.

evidence.quote: the median both overall as well as for each individual evaluator, is always 5, with average values never going below 4.3

claims.claim_text: Approximately 340 man-hours were spent creating the benchmarks for the no-resource languages.

evidence.quote: This required a total of ~340 man hours.
